items.title: Iran Active Conflict: 12% Oil Supply Shock = 9x the 1973 Crisis — Market Still Doesn't Believe It
items.idea: CPI core printed +0.2% (below +0.3% consensus), but SPY sold off anyway because the US resumed strikes on Iran after an Apache helicopter was downed over the Strait of Hormuz. Phil Dauber's commodity contact: the current 12% three-month supply shock is mathematically 9x the 1973 crisis that tripled oil prices. Yet Brent sits at ~$92. His ex-Marine contact's thesis: a two-week kinetic surge culminating in US troops landing on the Hormuz channel islands is how this ends. VIX hit 22.53 intraday. Trump has said 'deal is imminent' 37 times since March 1.
items.angle: The asymmetry is extreme: $92 oil implies no physical disruption risk. Any actual Strait disruption reprices dramatically. This is the dominant macro variable and equities are pricing it as background noise.
items.tweetA: CPI came in soft at +0.2%. Market sold off anyway. SPY doesn't care about CPI — it's trading the Strait of Hormuz. The 12% oil supply shock since February is mathematically 9x the size of the 1973 shock that tripled prices. Brent at $92. These are not symmetric outcomes.
items.tweetB: Trump has said 'deal is imminent' with Iran 37 times since March 1. Thirty-seven. A six-tour Marine's best sequencing of how this ends: US troops on the channel islands physically taking control of the Strait, then Iran negotiates. Brent at $92 is either correct or catastrophically wrong.

items.title: CPI 4.2% (Highest Since 2023), BTC Worst Week Since FTX, $1.7T Wiped From Gold/Silver
items.idea: CPI printed 4.2% — highest since 2023 — vindicating Michael Howell who had been warning for weeks. BTC posted its worst weekly performance since the FTX collapse (the exact previous bear-cycle bottom). South Korean markets hit circuit breakers. Institutions dumped Bitcoin at the fastest pace yet. The world's largest oil tankers are being ordered at a record pace. Gold and silver saw $1.7 trillion wiped from their market caps in mere hours. Fear & Greed Index at multi-year lows. Trump: Iran 'took too long' to make a deal.
items.angle: Stagflation shock: CPI accelerating while all assets sell off simultaneously. South Korean circuit breakers are a canary — when Asian markets start breaking, EM contagion follows.
items.tweetA: CPI 4.2%. Highest since 2023. BTC just had its worst week since FTX. South Korean markets hitting circuit breakers. Gold/silver lost $1.7T in hours. Everything is going down at once. This is what a stagflation shock looks like — not a gradual grind, a simultaneous repricing.
items.tweetB: Michael Howell called CPI 4.2% weeks ago and nobody listened. $BTC printing its worst week since the bear-cycle bottom. $1.7T wiped from gold and silver. Oil tankers ordered at record pace. The market doesn't have a single safe corner right now.

items.title: CFNAI Beats But Consumer Expectations Sub-80 for 16 Months — Joy/Pain Divergence Only Matched in Great Recession
items.idea: CFNAI April 0.14 beat the -0.03 consensus — best start to a year since 2021. But Conference Board Consumer Expectations sit at 74.4, below the recession-warning threshold of 80 for 16 consecutive months. The 'Fewer Jobs' z-score has been above +1 for 16 straight months — only matched during the Great Recession. P&I boost in CFNAI looks transitory (restock narrative); Dallas Fed Future Finished Goods Inventories fell to -2.9 in May, weakest since September 2025.
items.angle: Classic late-cycle divergence: hard production data positive, but forward-looking consumer confidence flashing deep recession signals. If Fewer Jobs expectations converge with hard data, it will be through unemployment rising — not confidence recovering.
items.tweetA: Consumer Expectations have been below 80 for 16 straight months. That is the Conference Board's recession threshold. During those same 16 months, CFNAI beat estimates. These two data series don't stay diverged forever. Historically, they close the gap by the hard data deteriorating.
items.tweetB: 'Fewer Jobs' expectations z-score above +1 for 16 consecutive months — a streak only matched during the Great Recession. Meanwhile CFNAI looks great. One of these is wrong. Guess which one tends to win.

items.title: SPY Downtrend Confirmed — 8 EMA Crossing 21 in 1-2 Sessions, Amazon 2022 Parallel for FCF-Negative Megacaps
items.idea: SPY gapped up and sold back to lows — first open below the 21 EMA in months. The 8 EMA is 1-2 sessions from crossing below the 21 EMA. Critical concern: if Iran-driven inflation persists while every hyperscaler turns FCF negative simultaneously, the Amazon 2022 parallel applies — Amazon dropped 50% when it went FCF negative as inflation was ramping. Roughly 40% of the market is megacaps. The 50-day MA creeping to $720 SPY is the likely target.
items.angle: FCF-negative hyperscalers + sticky inflation is the quantitative bear case for the AI trade. Small AI component plays are working; infrastructure layer is the risk. Bifurcation underway.
items.tweetA: 8 EMA crossing below 21 EMA on SPY in 1-2 sessions. Officially a downtrend. The Amazon 2022 parallel nobody is modeling: Amazon dropped 50% when it went FCF negative while inflation was ramping. Every hyperscaler is about to go FCF negative. CPI at 4.2%. Do the math.
items.tweetB: 40% of the market is megacaps all going FCF negative at the same time. The small AI components are ripping. The Mag 7 is selling off. Amazon 2022: capex cycle + inflation = 50% drawdown even with great business fundamentals. Nothing about that math has changed.

items.title: Indices Below 21-Day EMA: Red Flags Mounting — Risk Management, Not Aggression
items.idea: Following QQQ dropping >5% on June 9, all major indices are now below their 21-day EMA with red flags mounting. SixSigma had flagged June 8: 'low-volume bounce after high-volume sell-off — first bounce can't be trusted.' Recommend: tighten stops, no new capital until directional clarity, simple hedge via longer-term put options when S&P breaks below 21-day EMA.
items.angle: The 21 EMA call was made preemptively and proved right within 24 hours. Pattern: high-volume sell → low-volume bounce → continuation. This is the template for the next 1-2 weeks.
items.tweetA: SixSigma flagged June 8: 'low-volume bounce after high-volume sell-off — first bounce can't be trusted.' QQQ dropped 5% the next day. Now all indices are below their 21-day EMA. No prolonged market weakness in history has occurred above the 21 EMA. 100% of bear markets started below it.
items.tweetB: Simple framework that actually works in this tape: if S&P breaks below the 21-day EMA, buy longer-term puts. No macro thesis required. Just pattern recognition + discipline. The analysis is interesting; the setup is the trade.

items.title: VAR Shock on Friday Triggered Broad System Deleveraging — Equity Market Cleanup Underway
items.idea: Following Friday's VAR (Value-at-Risk) shock, Danny Dayan observes a systematic move to reduce leverage across all corners of the market, particularly equities. VAR shocks trigger mechanical forced selling as risk models across the financial system recalibrate simultaneously, amplifying drawdowns beyond fundamentals.
items.angle: VAR shock forces mechanical selling — not fundamental selling. This mechanism explains the gap-down and sell-on-bounce pattern. It also creates the eventual re-entry setup once leverage clears.
items.tweetA: Friday triggered a VAR shock. When risk models all recalibrate at once, selling is mechanical — not fundamental. The equity market right now isn't pricing news, it's pricing leverage unwinding. Two different things. One creates opportunity. One requires patience.
items.tweetB: Danny Dayan calling it 'The Cleanup.' VAR shock Friday triggers systemic deleveraging across all corners of equity markets. This is the part where even good stocks go down. The question isn't what's cheap. It's how much leverage is still left to unwind.

items.source: Not Boring / Pac Krishnaswami + Markie Wagner
items.title: Tokenmaxxing Was a Lab-Grown Supermeme — Fortune 500 CEOs Committed Billions, Got Nothing
items.idea: Markie Wagner (backed by Founders Fund, Kleiner Perkins, Genius Ventures, and OpenAI) argues tokenmaxxing — maximizing token consumption via dashboards — was a 'commercial form of AI psychosis.' Fortune 500 CEOs privately admit: 'we committed to all this token spend and have no idea what we are getting out of it.' Labs deployed agents like digital employees with unlimited credit cards, measured success by token leaderboards. The actual problem: AI implementation is 'too wasteful, too forgetful, too imprecise.'
items.angle: This is the narrative that cracks the enterprise AI hype cycle — a founder backed by OpenAI itself saying the prevailing implementation is wrong. The ROI problem is the bear case for inference demand growth acceleration.
items.tweetA: Founder backed by Founders Fund, Kleiner Perkins, and OpenAI said it publicly: tokenmaxxing was a commercial delusion. Fortune 500 CEOs committed billions to token spend and can't show ROI. 'Too wasteful, too forgetful, too imprecise.' The enterprise AI critique is now coming from inside the house.
items.tweetB: The labs sold enterprises on agents with no spending limits and measured success by token leaderboards. Markie Wagner calls it 'a lab-grown supermeme.' The smartest critique of the AI buildout isn't coming from skeptics. It's coming from someone OpenAI backed to build the replacement.

items.title: Everything is Recorded Now: New Enterprise Software Category Around Voice as the System of Record
items.idea: a16z argues AI is transforming enterprise through default recording of all work conversations. Key insight: onboard AI like employees — through osmosis in meetings, not reading docs. Bridgewater was prescient; OpenAI now records everything with agents standing in for senior leaders. Granola has better context on a16z culture than any other tool. New enterprise software category emerging around voice as system of record. Verbal cultures (Shopify, OpenAI) gain compounding advantage; written cultures (Stripe, Anthropic) already captured context by construction.
items.angle: Identifies the enterprise software white space: whoever builds the living context layer from voice/meetings wins a CRM-scale category. The venture signal points at a specific architectural wedge that is still early.
items.tweetA: Every meeting gets recorded now. Nobody voted on it — it just happened. a16z's insight: AI needs to be onboarded like a new employee — through osmosis in meetings, not by reading your docs. The company that builds the living context layer from all those recordings captures a CRM-scale market.
items.tweetB: Verbal cultures always lost institutional knowledge because meetings evaporate. Written cultures captured context by default. AI recording everything means Shopify and OpenAI now have the same compounding advantage Stripe and Anthropic had. The power dynamics of enterprise culture just shifted.

items.title: Ex-Millennium Traders Launch AI Commodities Fund: WASDE Parsed in Seconds, Wheat Went Limit Up
items.idea: Moreton Capital Partners (Les Finemore and Alistair Fullerton, ex-Farrer/Millennium) raised ~$100M through SMAs targeting $500M by year-end and $1B by spring 2027. Trading 100+ commodity markets. Thesis: commodities are informationally inefficient because processing the USDA's 40-page WASDE was too slow for humans. AI eliminates that latency. Proof: May WASDE showed big wheat production miss — AI flagged it bullish instantly, 'wheat went limit up and there was still time to position.' Building proprietary risk platform with AI explanations via Slack.
items.angle: Specific and testable thesis: commodities have less algorithmic competition than equities. AI narrows the information-processing gap. Dense government reports are alpha sources if you can read them in seconds.
items.tweetA: Commodity markets have been slow to adopt technology because processing the USDA's 40-page WASDE report in seconds wasn't feasible. AI makes it feasible. May WASDE: AI parsed it instantly, called a big production miss as bullish. Wheat went limit up and there was still time to position. $100M raised on that edge.
items.tweetB: Why build an AI hedge fund in commodities instead of equities? Because every equity already has 50 AI models on it. Orange juice futures don't. Les Finemore left Millennium to bet that less liquid, less analyzed markets are the last place where information edge still exists.

items.title: Hyperliquid HIP-3 is Winner-Takes-Most — Kinetiq's 'Launch' Bets on HIP-4, Not HIP-3
items.idea: On Hyperliquid's HIP-3 market, Trade.xyz has captured the liquidity — none have beaten it. Kinetiq (KNTQ) launches 'Launch' platform today to crowdfund new markets. Not attacking Trade.xyz through HIP-3 but positioning for HIP-4 mechanics (not yet defined). Kinetiq's core liquid HYPE staking business is shrinking. HYPE at $55 (-6%), BTC at $61.9k.
items.angle: Kinetiq is a bet on HIP-4 governance mechanics that don't yet exist. Venture-style speculation within an existing ecosystem. The HIP-4 governance timeline is the key watch variable.
items.tweetA: HIP-3 on Hyperliquid: Trade.xyz owns the liquidity, everyone else is fighting uphill. Kinetiq just launched a crowdfunding platform for new markets — not attacking Trade.xyz directly, but positioning for HIP-4. This is how Hyperliquid ecosystem plays actually work. $HYPE $KNTQ
items.tweetB: The DeFi perps narrative bifurcates: Trade.xyz dominates HIP-3, but every new protocol standard creates a new winner-takes-most race. $HYPE down 6% today. The question for $KNTQ isn't HIP-3 — it's whether HIP-4 gets defined, and when.

items.title: Bitcoin Corporate Treasury Cushion Is Shrinking — When Does It Flip From Support to Risk?
items.idea: Month of Bitcoin ETF outflows. Corporate Bitcoin treasuries (MicroStrategy-style) normally absorb weakness by continuing to accumulate — but this cushion is getting smaller. Critical question: if Bitcoin keeps falling, at what point do massive corporate BTC holdings stop acting as support and become a source of risk through forced selling, mark-to-market losses, or balance sheet stress?
items.angle: Corporate treasuries amplified the run-up and will amplify the drawdown if they flip from buyer to seller. This is the institutional contagion mechanism retail doesn't model.
items.tweetA: Corporate Bitcoin treasuries were supposed to be the permanent bid. Month of ETF outflows, and the corporate cushion is getting smaller. When does $BTC at $61.9k start creating balance sheet stress for the companies holding it as treasury? That is the reflexivity risk nobody is pricing.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin demand structure: ETFs (outflows), corporate treasuries (shrinking bid). You need new buyers to replace both. $BTC worst week since FTX. The two usual supports are weakening simultaneously. Ecoinometrics calling it correctly: this is fragility, not a floor.

items.title: Gravity L1 Mainnet Live: Parallel Execution + AptosBFT + Native Oracle + Agent Tooling From Day 1
items.idea: Gravity launches as a high-performance EVM L1: parallel execution via Grevm, pipelined AptosBFT consensus, native crosschain oracle via validator-attested external state, and 'Skills' installable agent tooling for AI-native dApps. Galxe migrates full application stack providing production load from day one. Structural risks: permissioned validator set (path to permissionless undefined), single-ecosystem dependency on Galxe, and oracle attack surface expanding with each source chain added.
items.angle: Agent-native tooling layer (Skills) is the differentiated angle vs. other L1 competitors. If AI agent use cases drive the next crypto cycle, chains with native agent infrastructure have first-mover advantage.
items.tweetA: New L1 worth watching: Gravity just went live with parallel execution, AptosBFT consensus, and native agent tooling called Skills baked in. Galxe migrated its entire stack — real transaction volume from day one, no cold-start problem. The agent-native infrastructure play is the differentiator.
items.tweetB: Gravity's risk matrix from Messari is honest: permissioned validators, single-ecosystem dependency on Galxe, and oracle attack surface grows with every chain added. All three are solvable. None are currently solved. The Galxe concentration is the one to watch.

items.title: CFTC Proposes New Prediction Markets Framework: Sports Betting In, War/Terrorism Contracts Out
items.idea: CFTC Chairman Michael Selig proposed a regulatory framework for prediction markets carving out sports betting while tightening limits on contracts tied to terrorism and war. More rulemaking signaled as coming. Creates clearer regulatory path for sports prediction markets while closing off controversial geopolitical contract categories.
items.angle: The regulatory moat is forming around sports betting prediction markets. Kalshi and Polymarket are about to diverge. War/terrorism exclusion means Polymarket's most-watched products may face headwinds.
items.tweetA: CFTC Chairman just proposed carving out sports betting prediction markets while banning war/terrorism contracts. Kalshi and Polymarket are about to diverge. The sports betting prediction market — multi-billion dollar TAM — just got its clearest regulatory path ever.
items.tweetB: Prediction markets getting segmented by CFTC: sports = legal path, war/terrorism = tighter limits. The most interesting data gets regulated. The most monetizable gets blessed. Regulators choosing revenue over information. Classic playbook.

items.title: MNX: $6.4M Pre-Seed for AI-Economy Futures Exchange — Compute Perps, Lab Valuation Futures, AI Prediction Markets
items.idea: Manifold Markets co-founders raised $6.4M pre-seed for MNX, a MegaETH-based futures exchange for AI-economy assets. Products: compute perps (trade GPU/AI compute as a futures contract), private lab valuation futures (synthetic Anthropic/OpenAI pre-IPO exposure), and AI-specific prediction markets. Mainnet launching this summer.
items.angle: Compute perps are the most intellectually interesting product — trading the fundamental input cost of the AI economy as a derivative. Could become the energy futures of the AI era.
items.tweetA: MNX is building futures on AI-economy assets: compute perps, private lab valuation futures, AI prediction markets. $6.4M pre-seed from Manifold Markets co-founders on MegaETH, mainnet this summer. Trade the cost of compute directly. Most interesting crypto x AI product announced this week.
items.tweetB: The most direct bet on AI infrastructure isn't NVDA or the hyperscalers — it's the cost of compute itself, as a derivative. MNX is building compute perps. Mainnet this summer. TAM: anyone hedging AI infrastructure costs. That list is getting very long.

items.title: CoreWeave Insiders Dumped $2.3B in Stock Since IPO — Jane Street Angle Under Investigation
items.idea: CoreWeave executives have dumped $2.3 billion in stock since the IPO. Herb Greenberg is now investigating a 'Jane Street edition' angle — Jane Street has a connection to CoreWeave that he considers worth scrutinizing. Full details behind paywall, but $2.3B in insider selling + Jane Street involvement is the red flag signal.
items.angle: $2.3B in insider selling is extraordinary for a recently IPO'd AI infrastructure company. Jane Street's involvement adds a layer of financial engineering sophistication worth understanding. Potential short thesis in development.
items.tweetA: CoreWeave insiders dumped $2.3 billion in stock since the IPO. That is an extraordinary number. And Herb Greenberg is now investigating the Jane Street angle — the most sophisticated trading firm in the world has a connection to this company. These two facts together deserve scrutiny.
items.tweetB: While everyone watches SpaceX and Anthropic IPOs, $CRWV executives sold $2.3B since listing. Herb Greenberg is on the Jane Street thread. When the smartest money is both connected to AND selling, that is the setup worth examining. Greenberg builds good short theses.

items.title: Wheat Crop Ratings Drop to Lowest Ever as Hormuz Conflict Spreads to Agricultural Supply Chain
items.idea: Bloomberg headline flagged by Dauber: 'Wheat Gains as US Crop Ratings Unexpectedly Drop to Lowest Ever.' The Hormuz conflict is bleeding into agricultural supply chains beyond energy. When global shipping routes are disrupted, food costs follow energy costs with a lag. CPI could have an additional leg beyond energy — food inflation layering on top of an already 4.2% print.
items.angle: The 1973 oil shock created a food crisis as the second-order effect. Current shock is mathematically 9x bigger. Wheat crop ratings at all-time lows. If CPI is at 4.2% now, the food component hasn't fully hit yet.
items.tweetA: Buried in today's Hormuz conflict story: US wheat crop ratings just dropped to the lowest ever recorded. Bloomberg flagged it. Not just energy — the agricultural supply chain is now on the same conflict clock. CPI at 4.2% looks like the start of something, not the peak.
items.tweetB: The 1973 oil shock created a food crisis as the second-order effect. Current supply shock is 9x bigger. Wheat crop ratings hit all-time lows today. When shipping routes are disrupted, food costs follow energy costs with a lag. The sequence is playing out in real time.

items.title: ARK Venture Fund Invests in SonaThera Series B: Non-Viral Gene Therapy Delivery Platform
items.idea: ARK Venture Fund invested in SonaThera's Series B. SonaThera is a US-based biotech developing a non-viral gene therapy delivery platform and its own pipeline of genetic medicines. Non-viral gene therapy delivery has been the field's holy grail — AAV vectors have manufacturing constraints, immune response issues, and cargo size limits. A working non-viral platform could dramatically expand the addressable patient population for genetic medicines.
items.angle: ARK Ventures investments signal thematic conviction in enabling technologies. Non-viral gene therapy is potentially the platform layer for all of personalized medicine. If SonaThera's delivery platform works at scale, it could be the Illumina of the gene therapy era.
items.tweetA: ARK Venture just invested in SonaThera Series B: a non-viral gene therapy delivery platform. Viral vectors (AAV) have immune response problems and manufacturing constraints. A working non-viral platform unlocks genetic medicine at scale. One of the most important enabling technologies in biotech.
items.tweetB: The bottleneck in gene therapy is not the genes — it is the delivery system. AAV vectors are expensive, limited in cargo size, and trigger immune responses. SonaThera's non-viral platform, now ARK-backed, is attacking this constraint directly. If it works, the addressable patient population expands by an order of magnitude.

items.title: Oracle Earnings Tonight: Full Slide Deck — Larry Ellison's Big Bet on OCI as the AI Cloud
items.idea: Capital Flows published a full slide deck with Oracle fundamentals, scenario analysis, and framing around Larry Ellison's big bet ahead of Oracle earnings tonight. Oracle has been aggressively building OCI and positioning against AWS/Azure/GCP. Oracle's cloud revenue accelerated as hyperscalers outsourced overflow compute to OCI at scale. Key earnings print for AI infrastructure demand signal — coinciding with SPY breaking below its 21 EMA.
items.angle: Oracle earnings in a down tape is a binary risk event. A cloud beat validates AI infrastructure demand continues. A miss cascades into broader hyperscaler re-rating, compounding the megacap pressure underway.
items.tweetA: Oracle earnings tonight. Larry Ellison bet the company on OCI as the AI infrastructure overflow play. In a week where $SPY broke its 21 EMA and $BTC had its worst week since FTX, an ORCL cloud beat could be the only thing standing between here and a real air pocket in tech.
items.tweetB: Everyone watches $MSFT $AMZN $GOOGL for AI cloud signals. Meanwhile $ORCL quietly built OCI into the relief valve when hyperscalers were capacity-constrained. Earnings tonight tell us if that demand was real or pull-forward. Larry Ellison's biggest bet in 30 years, in the worst tape of 2026.
